sentences.text: Yes, on the cart, on the mattress, and my father pushing, my mother walking behind.
My mother looked pretty thin, even though we had had something to eat there.
But my mother looked still fairly thin.
And so I don't think they took too much notice of us somehow.
We looked a bit bedraggled looking, and we didn't look like anything that was-- and also, because we had a cart.
We just weren't walking, we had some belongings on it.
So we eventually had to cross a bridge, where there was a guard.
And there were like army personnel standing there.
And-- and my father had said, look, all these papers, I've got, so don't worry, and just-- just, you know, look at them straight in the face.
Look-- just look.
My mother had blue eyes, too.
But my mother's hair was a little darker.
And-- but he said to me, [?
Corrie, ?] when they look at you, or they say anything, just smile.
Laugh, laugh.

So we got to do the bridge, and there was a whole lot-- in that Haarlemmermeer, where we were going, there was the food stores were being-- like, there were stores from the farmers, where they had, like, big storage area where they-- the-- the army also used to come and take their food from.
So there was a lot of transport going across that bridge, and they were checking regularly-- checkpoint there.

But somehow, it was just at the time-- it must have been our luck-- it was just at the time when all that transportation was going on, and they were checking all the people, because sometimes they were checking in the trucks, big trucks, to see if they were hiding anybody in amongst the food and things, in bales of things, hay and whatever.

So as this was our luck, as this was going on, the fellow was so interested to see that everything was going all right for the big trucks, and whatever was going on around him, that when he saw us three bedraggled looking people, I suppose, he just-- my father had his papers out, his card, that said Johannes [?
Peter Bink ?] and where we was coming from, and he had to go there.
So he just marched on, and-- and he looked at me, and I, you know, smiled, and sort of did exactly what I was told, and we marched along with the cart, and my mother sort of looking down.
And I remember thinking at the time, if we make it across the bridge, we'll make it, you know.
And once we got that first stop down, we made it.
So we got to this family, Mien and Jan Baay.
And my father had been there already once, so he knew what he was.
And they were nice people, very nice.
It was distant relative, as I say, because of my grandmother's side of the family.
They was, like, a very distant, cousin of a cousin, who was married to a non-Jewish man, and who had had children, so the children were not Jewish at all.
And it was like a big thing.
And they said, yes, we will keep you.
Now, they lived out on a sort of a farm land, a bit isolated, so we could-- they could see if anybody was coming.
But they said, Simon, we have a big problem.
You can stay.
We've got two children-- they had two children younger than me-- but you are the man that must supply us with food, because Jan can't do it.
They won't give him.
You have to see if you can't-- seeing that you've got some contact-- that you can't get some food for us.
We got-- we're starving here.
We are happy to have you.
We can't only just eat tulip bulbs.
We-- we are only eating tulip bulbs.
Yes.
Oh, yes, we were all there.
We were all in the room.
And she said, it's an impossibility for us.
We're finding very scarcity of food, and you can-- you must try and be the one that provides us with food.
That is the only condition that we can live here like this.
So my father said, I'll do it.
I-- my mother looked at him, and I remember thinking, how-- how's he going to do it, you know?
But he got himself a bike from the Jan, and he went to all the surrounding farms.
Every so often when there was a clearage, like-- because the army was not far off, the army base with their food stores, some of these farmers still had food.
So he went, and he-- he never actually ever told me exactly how we did it.
But I remember the first week or so we ate tulip bulbs galore.
We ate, and we made syrup out of them, and we ate tulip bulbs, and we boiled them like potatoes.
And I-- we said it was still not bad.
A bit sweet.
I still taste it a little bit.
A little bit sickly sweet.
And we made like-- if you-- if you boiled it and boiled and boiled it, it became like a syrup, like a honey.
And sugar-- sugar beet we ate, sugar beet.
And-- anyway, it was no good, because we had to have some other staple food.
So my father went, and the first day he came back after two weeks of all this eating this.
He had beans with him.
Brown beans, kidney beans.
And my mother, I remember getting a-- getting a big [? soaking-- ?] a big sauce pan, and she said, now, you can't eat these like this.
Because I said, can I eat these?
And she said, no, you've got to soak them at least 24 hours.
And then we'll boil them, and then we'll have them.
And then my father got some potatoes, and, like, slowly, little bit, little bit, the family kept themselves going.
But it was still-- the nourishment was poor.
But we kept going.
One day got a bag of flour, and my mother made bread.
Didn't rise very much, because there was no yeast, but we-- I remember, like, you know, and I said, oh fantastic, like, [? with ?] bread.
And-- but slowly, slowly, it-- it became a bit bad, because there wasn't enough to go around.
The children needed-- the little children needed the food more than anything.
My mother gave me, and the little children, and the parents sort of ate what they could, and left for the children.
So then one day, my mother woke up, and she said she was feeling very sick, and she could hardly get up.
She was ill, really ill.
And they said they have to take her to a hospital.
And there was a hospital where they were only treating-- the staff was underground.
The whole-- the whole hospital was like a hospital for people that were not supposed to be there.
So they took them-- took her there.
And I remember my mother going, and she couldn't-- ah, she couldn't stand.
She was malnutritioned, she was finished.
And she-- looked like skeleton.
And my father also, you know, was looking pretty bad.
But I think he had somehow or the other been able to sort of cope with it.
He looked awful, but he was all right.
I couldn't see it, because I was with them all the time.
But I knew that when my mother went to hospital, it was bad.
Anyway, it was-- by that time, it was about-- I turned nine in February, 2nd of February.
I think by that time my mother told me.
By that time, I think she used to say-- oh, I think-- I think she must have-- my mother must have had some information.
They got underground information.
Somehow, they knew where the army, where the-- where the-- you know, what was happening in England, what was happening, they got certain news things through.
How?
I don't know exactly how they worked it, because I was a child.
I wasn't supposed to know those things.
But they got information, and I know that when it was my-- like I turned nine, and I remember my-- my mother going to hospital.
I turned in February, and my mother was sick, sick, sick.
And by May, it started to wind down a little bit.
We got information that certain parts in-- of Holland, Germans had withdrawn.
Certain parts.
And they said, you know, looks like things might be easing off.

And then, one day in May, there was tremendous noise about the roofs of our-- where-- the house, and we-- my dad looked and I looked, and there were airplanes, and there were all sorts of things going on.
And-- and we said, oh my god, you know, what's happening here?
Like, we thought maybe we were being bombed.
And no, it was the Americans, American planes flying over.
And really, the field, they scattered parcels of all sorts.
I mean, we were running.
We were-- Dad, myself, Jan Mien, all the children, all running into the field, and seeing.
And there were loaves of bread.
But also, there was things like tins, tin food, bully beef.
All sorts of food that we couldn't open, but the bread-- as I-- there were loaves of white bread.
They were this high, like ten cloves that you can get now, you know those big ones?
And they were white, fluffy, I've never seen bread like that, never.
I'll never see it again, because it was the first time I've seen bread in five years, like that kind of bread.
And I mean, it was something amazing to me, and I started eating this bread as I'm-- I'm walking with it.
Eating, and eating, and eating, and eating, and eating, by the time I got to the house, I was violently ill.
I vomited all over the shoal.
I was so ill, because I couldn't digest.
I mean, you don't do that, you know.
But nobody stopped me, and nobody-- nobody said anything, and everybody just sort of did their own thing.
And they said, it doesn't matter, doesn't matter, if you're sick, you're sick, you'll be all right, but you shouldn't have eaten so much.
I stuffed it down.
I was stuffing it down.
And the very sad part of this was that my mother, who had waited for this so much, who had always said to me and to my father, we'll make it, we'll make it, wasn't there.
She was nowhere there to share that, to share that emotion of, you know, we are free.
And it was a very sad thing, really, because I didn't know whether she was going to come out of the hospital.
They did-- they had diagnosed tuberculosis, is that she was malnutritioned.
And it was a freedom that was sort of, for a moment, very sad.
I kept on saying, I want my mother, I want mother to share this.
But we had to wait.
We couldn't start running on the road, because there were still people out that would have shot.
There were still people, [INAUDIBLE], that were people that were collaborators, Dutch nationals that were collaborators with the Germans, that would have just shot.
Because now came the time that they were running scared, and they were now the frightened ones, and they would have just killed anybody in their path.
So we had to wait.
And eventually, I remember, a few weeks later-- we waited with this Jan and Mien Baay, and it was tremendous.
When my mother came home, she was-- had to have special-- she had special coupons for milk, and for all this for herself, because she had to have nourishment.
So many eggs, so many-- I mean, it was amazing.
When the war had ended, it was the 5th of May, and everything sort of had come to a stop.
But she came out a few weeks later, and, you know, that was the beginning and the end of a whole new life.
The end of the war, and now came the struggle where are we going to live?
What are we going to do?
And that was the next episode in our life, because it was not easy.
We were allocated a home in Haamstede of the [NON-ENGLISH].
It was a home of a German collaborator.
He also had been very much involved with the Nazi party.
They had thrown him out of there.
They put him in jail.
And they allocated all the people that were homeless, Jews and non-Jews, but more Jews than non in the beginning, because we were a little bit more so.
That was government.
Government of Holland.
Allocated to people that were homeless.
And they gave us a home to live in.
And that was in Haamstede of the [NON-ENGLISH].
And that was marvelous.
I mean, that was our first home that we said we're going to stay here now for a while.
Now, we'll find out what to do, but it was-- it was somewhere to go to.
And that was actually a very nice ending for-- for-- or beginning, because it gave me a stability with my parents.
Together, we moved into a place where the three of us had a unit, and we were there together.
And-- and that gave me, you know, a good feeling.

And then, a few-- my mother and father had hardly settled in, maybe six months, when they decided that there were so many Jewish children that had lost their parents, and their parents had gone, so they said, would you like a brother?
And I said, yes, I would like a brother.
I'd like a brother.
I'd like very much so.
My mother had always told-- told me that she had a brother, and he died during the war.
But I said, yes, I'd like it.
And we-- they went to the Red Cross to have a look if they could find somebody, the-- a child, that was sort of accessible to us, to see if they could give a child a home and adopt it.
And when they went there, they were looking through the lists, and they discovered a name which came very familiar to them.
And it said, Herman Lezer.
And they thought, my god, this is something.
My mother-- father's sister had a son, which was my first cousin, and her husband.
They were sent to concentration camp.
He escaped.
They-- he was sent into hiding.
And this is the little boy, [INAUDIBLE], who was my first cousin, whom was still alive.
And they decided to go and find-- find him.
And when they did, they found him on a farm in Friesland, in Holland, with farmers, who had very, very decent Christian family.
Very, very nice family.
And they said that they were the only survivors there, like my father and mother.
That was their aunt-- his uncle and aunt.
And they told me about it, that they were going to see him.
And then, they brought him home.
The people said that they would gladly give him up to family, gladly, because he-- that was our flesh and blood.
But they were very sad to see him go.
They called him [DUTCH], because he was like a brother for-- for the family, for the other children, and we brought-- my parents brought him home.
And he only spoke Friesland dialect.
He was a lovely little boy, six years old.
He'd been-- lost his parents.
And so he became my brother.
He was my first cousin who became my brother, and I had a companion for the first time in my life.
I had a brother.
I was very happy about.
And then, you know-- do you want to ask me something?
Well, could you tell me just about how-- if you moved from where you lived?
Did you continue to live there?
No, we didn't continue to live there.
We-- after a little while, we discovered that my auntie had survived Auschwitz, so she and my grandmother came to stay with us in Haamstede.
And my parents wanted to go back to Amsterdam to see if they could set up business again.
So they decided they would leave the house to my auntie, and my grandmother, and her new husband.
She married again.
And we went to Amsterdam.
A lot of movement to place all the time.
There was always something.
But we went to Amsterdam, and we set up another business there, my father and my mother, and they worked very hard.
But the happiness that-- my grandfather was alive, and my uncle-- but the happiness that they were looking for, they had-- they found me, and they find [?
Harry, ?] which was marvelous.
And I had another little brother after the war.
He was born in 1948, and it was marvelous.
His name is Fred Swaab.
And they decided they wanted to make a life somewhere else, all together, because the memories of Holland, even though we tried to make it, and we-- they worked hard, they were not good.
And all my father's family was gone.
They were all killed in concentration camps.
His mother and his five sisters.
And his-- my grandmother from my father's side had 10 brothers and sisters who were all killed in concentration camps.
My mother's side of the family, she had the brother that she always spoke about, and a wife and a nine months old baby, they were all killed in concentration camps.
She had a whole lot of cousins, aunties and uncles, all killed in concentration camps.
So the place was empty.
Amsterdam was empty.
And they decided they want to go and try it somewhere else, and set up something new for me, and for my brothers.
And so, we decided to come to Australia.
Yes, I-- I attended school, which was very difficult, in Haamstede.
I-- I was nine and a bit years old, and I started for the first time in school, doing first class.
And I had never read or done anything, sums or anything like that.
So my mother had taught me a little bit, the basics.
Just a, you know, little bit-- little bit of writing, little bit-- but nothing much, as a child.
And then I went to Amsterdam, and I-- in Amsterdam I found that there was a mixture of children in the school.
There were boys and girls with me that were like from, I would say, nearly 10 in Amsterdam, and there were boys of 14, 13, girls, 12, nine, all learning.
Everybody had lost their schooling, so we were all sitting in-- in second class, all on one level.
Big boys, little children, and everybody together.
And I said-- my parents said, look you've got to do your best, because we want you to do well.
So I went, and I tried to do it, and I did it.
I did two classes every year.
In other words, when I did, like, first and second, third and fourth, you know, like that, fifth and sixth.
And then that was very good, because then I was supposed to go to high school.
I was nearly 13 when I was supposed to go to high school, and I made it.
Normally it's 12, but I made it, which was good.
Now, we had a headmaster that was an antisemite.
After all this, we discovered there was one headmaster in that school that was a terrible antisemite.
And he had decided that [?
Corrie ?] Swaab was not going to high school, because she had skipped too many classes.
She'd done too many in the three years.
I did three years when normal people do six.
And my parents said, but she has sat for the test, and she will go.
She will go to high school.
She's done enough, had enough trouble, that she's not going to now go backwards, and you hold her back, because it's not fair.
So they said-- he said no.
And my father said, then she will sit for another test under the government, and I will make sure that she will get that test.
Because my father, by that time, wasn't taking any more nonsense.
He'd had enough.
So this man was very hard, and I got a government test, and I sat for it, and I passed it.
And I went to high school.
And it showed you that there's a peculiar thing in the world.
Even though they can see hardship has been-- it's been so hard, and this man was still holding a little girl back, at the age of 13.
But it worked, and I went to high school.
And I did what you call intermediate here.
I did only three years of high school, because we came to Australia.
And I finished the intermediate, and we came to Australia.
I was nearly 16.
Nearly.
I was sad.
I-- I didn't want to leave, because I just made friends.
I had just made teenage friends.
And when my parents said, I think we will make the move, because it's never going to change in Holland.
Looks like there's always going to be this.
When we saw this now with this headmaster, we saw this, we saw this, we're never going to end up with a different thing.
We must make a new life for you children.
So I said, Dad, you know, this girlfriend, that girlfriend.
He said, you'll make new girlfriends again.
And you'll get a good life, and sunshine.
Australia has got beautiful sunshine, beautiful beaches.
Something that you-- and I won't let you work hard, he said.
You know what, if it's a good day, and you can go to the beach, you can sit in the sun, and you can sit on the beach.
I won't care if you don't earn any money.
We will see how we do it.
And that's what happened.
We came, and he really spoiled me, too.
He-- he-- we came to Australia, and I said I-- I will always be together with my family.
I would have gone through fire to be with them.
I would never have left, or said, I don't want to stay with-- or go with them, because that was everything for me, to have that family unit.
And still, up till the end, it still was.
My mother lived to be 81, and I miss her every day, because that unit was there.
